preamble: The relationship between the glottal stop and the alveolar flap is frequently observed in allophonic competition, particularly in English dialects where both sounds serve as lenited versions of the phoneme /t/.

examples.note: Productive 'T-to-R' rule where word-final /t/ becomes a rhotic flap before a vowel, replacing the glottal stop found in other dialects.
In some sociolects, the flap is used as a 'middle ground' between formal [t] and stigmatized glottal [ʔ].

sources: Wells, J. C. (1982). Accents of English.
Honeybone, P. (2001). Lenition inhibition, contrast preservation and 'T-to-R' in Liverpool English.
